CECalvin Ells

Today

You said:
how do i work with workspaces in EDGE
Copilot said:
Copilot

You can use Workspaces in Microsoft Edge to group tabs by project or focus area so you can switch context quickly without losing your place. Here’s the practical, step‑by‑step way to work with them.

What Edge Workspaces are

Edge Workspaces let you organize related tabs into separate browser spaces (for example, “OMAX migration,” “Performance investigation,” or “Personal research”). Each workspace opens in its own Edge window and remembers your tabs exactly as you left them, which helps reduce tab clutter and context switching.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

Create a workspace

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Select the Search tabs icon (top‑left) and choose Manage workspaces.
  3. Select New Workspace and choose either:
    • Create workspace with X tabs (from what’s currently open), or
    • Create blank workspace.
  4. Name the workspace, pick a color/theme, and select Done.
  5. Open any tabs you want—those tabs now belong to that workspace.

Each workspace opens in its own Edge window automatically.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

Switch between workspaces

  • Select the Workspaces icon (top‑left of Edge).
  • Choose the workspace you want to open.

Workspaces are listed by most recently used, making it easy to jump back into active projects.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

Edit a workspace

You can change the name or color at any time:

  1. Open the Workspaces menu.
  2. Select More options (…) next to a workspace.
  3. Choose Edit workspace, update details, and select Done.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

Delete a workspace

  1. Open the Workspaces menu.
  2. Select More options (…) next to the workspace.
  3. Choose Delete workspace, then confirm.

Deleting a workspace removes the workspace container, not the underlying sites themselves.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

Syncing and requirements (important)

To keep workspaces available across devices:

  • You must be signed into Edge with a Microsoft or work account.
  • Sync must be enabled, including Workspaces and Tab Groups in Edge settings.
  • Workspaces require Microsoft Edge version 144 or later and are supported on desktop (Windows, macOS).Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

Key limitations to be aware of

  • The new Edge Workspaces are personal only—sharing or inviting others is not supported.
  • Locking tabs inside a workspace is not supported.
  • Collaboration features from older workspace versions are no longer available.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

Practical tip for dev work

Given how you juggle multiple technical threads, a strong pattern is:

  • One workspace per project or investigation
  • Keep “long‑running” tabs (docs, dashboards, CMS admin) inside the relevant workspace
  • Close the entire workspace when you’re done for the day instead of micromanaging tabs

If you want, I can help you design a workspace naming + color system that matches how you structure projects and investigations today.

Sources

You said:
Help me design a workspace naming and color system
Copilot said:
Copilot

Great—let’s make this fast to scan and consistent, using the two things Edge Workspaces actually gives you: a name + a theme color, and the ability to edit them anytime.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

1) What Edge Workspaces supports (so we design to it)

  • You can create a workspace, give it a name, and choose a theme (color) when you make it.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces
  • You can edit the workspace name/theme later from the Workspaces menu.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces
  • Workspaces appear in the menu in most-recently-used order (not a custom sort), so the name needs to be readable at a glance.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces
  • If you want them across devices, enable sync for Workspaces and Tab Groups.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

2) Naming system (simple, scannable, and durable)

The format (recommended)

<Lane> | <Project> | <Intent> (<Env>)

Where:

  • Lane = 3–5 letters that categorize the work (keeps things grouped mentally even though Edge sorts MRU)
  • Project = short project name
  • Intent = what you’re doing in there (build, debug, QA, doc, research, ops)
  • Env (optional) = PROD / STG / DEV / LOCAL

Standard “Lane” codes (pick 6–8 and stick to them)

Here’s a set tuned for the kinds of work I see in your artifacts (migration/devops/marketing/perf/AI research):

  • MIGR = migrations / cutovers
  • CMS = CMS editing/dev/build work
  • MKTG = D365/marketing forms/journeys
  • PERF = web performance and analysis
  • OPS = devops, pipelines, monitoring, redirects/SSL, incident response
  • R&D = research/spikes (AI, prototypes)
  • MEET = “meeting support” workspace (agenda docs, notes, links)

Rule of thumb: If you can’t decide the Lane in 2 seconds, use OPS or R&D.

Intent verbs (keep a fixed vocabulary)

Use one of these so your brain doesn’t have to parse novel wording:

  • Build, Debug, QA, Ship, Doc, Review, Research, Monitor

Examples (based on your current work)

  • MIGR | OMAX | Build (STG) — pulled from your migration tracker notes in OMAX Migration. [2](https://hypertherm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/calvin_ells_hypertherm_com/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?action=edit&mobileredirect=true&wdorigin=Sharepoint&DefaultItemOpen=1&sourcedoc={363793dc-1f6a-4a53-b717-6c4853768d76}&wd=target(/1 GTD/Current.one/)&wdpartid={faef1a65-87eb-0465-1096-28c22d895aae}{1}&wdsectionfileid={78244c42-531f-4bc9-b3c3-9606b37ee80b})
  • CMS | Optimizely | Debug (LOCAL) — you’re actively working in local Optimizely edit + render tabs in render.sharepoint
  • R&D | Opal | Research — matches your notes in OPAL and the discussion meeting Optimizely // Hyperthem: Opal Discussion. [4](https://hypertherm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/calvin_ells_hypertherm_com/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?action=edit&mobileredirect=true&wdorigin=Sharepoint&DefaultItemOpen=1&sourcedoc={363793dc-1f6a-4a53-b717-6c4853768d76}&wd=target(/1 GTD/Current.one/)&wdpartid={1df770a3-0182-0355-2cb2-b34a287f2a51}{1}&wdsectionfileid={78244c42-531f-4bc9-b3c3-9606b37ee80b})Optimizely // Hyperthem: Opal Discussion
  • OPS | Redirects/SSL | Monitor (PROD) — aligns with the “SSL/cname/redirects” discussion captured in Leads & Redirects-20260513_080541-Meeting Recording.Leads & Redirects-20260513_080541-Meeting Recording
  • MEET | Web DevOps | Notes — anchored to Weekly Web DevOps Meeting.Weekly Web DevOps Meeting
  • MKTG | OMAX D365 | Ship (PROD) — matches the “OMAX D365 Implementation” goal set in Goals.Goals

3) Color system (one glance = one meaning)

Because names can get truncated, color should encode urgency / risk / environment. The cleanest approach is to map colors to Lanes:

Lane → Color mapping (recommended)

  • Red = OPS (PROD / incidents / hotfix / SSL / outages)
  • Orange = MIGR (cutover / launch / high coordination)
  • Blue = CMS (day-to-day build/debug)
  • Green = PERF (measure, optimize, monitor)
  • Purple = R&D (spikes, AI, prototypes)
  • Teal = MKTG (D365 forms/journeys/campaigns)
  • Gray = MEET (meeting notes/support)

If you want an even simpler system: Red=PROD, Yellow/Orange=STG, Blue=DEV, Gray=Admin/Notes, regardless of Lane. (But the Lane-based system tends to be more useful day to day.)

Optional: encode environment in the name too

If you frequently juggle multiple environments, append:

  • (PROD) / (STG) / (DEV) / (LOCAL)

That gives you both color and text as redundant signals (hard to misread during a rush).

4) Quick “cheat sheet” you can paste into a note

Naming: LANE | Project | Intent (Env)
Lane codes: MIGR, CMS, MKTG, PERF, OPS, R&D, MEET
Colors: OPS=Red, MIGR=Orange, CMS=Blue, PERF=Green, R&D=Purple, MKTG=Teal, MEET=Gray

5) How to apply this in Edge (2-minute setup)

  1. Open the Workspaces menu (top-left).Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces
  2. For an existing workspace: select More options (…) → Edit workspace → update name and theme.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces
  3. Turn on sync for Workspaces and Tab Groups if you want them across devices.Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces

6) A maintenance loop that keeps it clean (no extra friction)

  • Weekly: delete or archive any workspace whose Intent is “Ship/QA” and is done (keeps the MRU list meaningful).Getting started with Microsoft Edge Workspaces
  • Daily: if you open a workspace and it’s “the wrong color,” fix it immediately—your future self will trust the system more than the text.

If you tell me whether you prefer Lane-based colors (OPS/MIGR/CMS…) or Environment-based colors (PROD/STG/DEV), I’ll tailor the final mapping and give you a tight set of 8 names that match your active streams like OMAX Migration and Weekly Web DevOps Meeting. [2](https://hypertherm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/calvin_ells_hypertherm_com/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?action=edit&mobileredirect=true&wdorigin=Sharepoint&DefaultItemOpen=1&sourcedoc={363793dc-1f6a-4a53-b717-6c4853768d76}&wd=target(/1 GTD/Current.one/)&wdpartid={faef1a65-87eb-0465-1096-28c22d895aae}{1}&wdsectionfileid={78244c42-531f-4bc9-b3c3-9606b37ee80b})Weekly Web DevOps Meeting

You said:
Lane-based colors seem more appropriate
Copilot said:
Copilot

Perfect. Let’s lock in a lane‑based system that’s consistent, low‑friction, and optimized for quick visual parsing in Edge.

Below is the final recommended spec plus concrete examples tailored to how you work.

1. Final model (one sentence rule)

Color = what kind of work this is.
Name = what project + what you’re doing right now.

This keeps decisions fast and avoids over‑thinking.

2. Standard lane set (keep this fixed)

I strongly recommend 7 lanes max. Fewer lanes = faster recognition.

LaneColorMeaningWhen to use it
OPS🔴 RedProduction / risk / breakageIncidents, SSL, redirects, hotfixes, PROD monitoring
MIGR🟠 OrangeBig coordinated changeSite migrations, cutovers, launch prep
CMS🔵 BlueCore dev / CMS workOptimizely dev, templates, content modeling
PERF🟢 GreenMeasurement & optimizationCWV, Lighthouse, perf audits
MKTG🟦 TealMarketing systemsD365 forms, journeys, attribution
R&D🟣 PurpleExploration & spikesAI/Opal work, experiments, prototypes
MEET⚪ GrayMeetings & notesAgendas, decks, notes, follow‑ups

✅ These lanes map cleanly to how you already separate work mentally
✅ Color choice emphasizes risk & urgency first (red/orange)

3. Naming format (final)

LANE | Project | Intent (Env)

Rules

  • LANE is always first (caps, fixed vocabulary)
  • Project is short (1–2 words)
  • Intent is a verb from a fixed list
  • Env is optional but recommended when relevant

Approved intent verbs

Use only these to keep things predictable:

  • Build
  • Debug
  • QA
  • Ship
  • Research
  • Review
  • Monitor
  • Notes

4. Concrete examples (mapped to your real work)

Migration / launch

  • 🟠 MIGR | OMAX | Build (STG)
  • 🟠 MIGR | OMAX | QA
  • 🟠 MIGR | OMAX | Cutover (PROD)

CMS / dev

  • 🔵 CMS | Optimizely | Build (LOCAL)
  • 🔵 CMS | Optimizely | Debug
  • 🔵 CMS | Global Templates | Review

Ops / prod safety

  • 🔴 OPS | Redirects & SSL | Monitor
  • 🔴 OPS | Forms | Debug (PROD)
  • 🔴 OPS | GA Tracking | Verify

Performance

  • 🟢 PERF | Hypertherm.com | Audit
  • 🟢 PERF | Mobile CLS | Fix
  • 🟢 PERF | CWV | Monitor

Marketing / D365

  • 🟦 MKTG | OMAX D365 | Build
  • 🟦 MKTG | Forms | QA
  • 🟦 MKTG | Attribution | Review

R&D / AI

  • 🟣 R&D | Opal | Research
  • 🟣 R&D | AI Wiki | Prototype
  • 🟣 R&D | Prompt System | Design

Meetings / notes

  • ⚪ MEET | Web DevOps | Notes
  • ⚪ MEET | Optimizely | Prep
  • ⚪ MEET | Launch Review | Notes

5. Visual heuristics (how your brain will read this)

  • Red window visible? → “Production attention required”
  • Orange window open? → “Careful, coordinated change”
  • Blue window stack? → “Normal dev flow”
  • Purple creep? → “Time‑boxed research—don’t let it sprawl”

This works even when names are truncated or you’re moving fast.

6. Lifecycle rules (this keeps it clean long‑term)

Daily

  • If intent changes → rename the workspace immediately
    (“Build” → “Debug” → “Ship”)

Weekly

  • Delete or close any workspace whose intent is Ship, QA, or Monitor and is done

Always

  • Never reuse a workspace for a different lane
    (Color drift = trust erosion)

7. One‑line cheat sheet (paste this somewhere)

Color = Lane (OPS red, MIGR orange, CMS blue, PERF green, MKTG teal, R&D purple, MEET gray)
Name = LANE | Project | Intent (Env)

If you want, next we can:

  • Design a “default workspace set” you spin up every Monday
  • Or align this system with VS Code workspaces + folders
  • Or build a kill rule (“When to delete vs keep a workspace”)

Just tell me where you want to take it.

  • ✅ Predictable headings → works well with Obsidian/Logseq/Notion/SIEM-style wiki indexing

Suggested next integrations (optional)

Edge Workspace Naming & Color System

Today

You said:
how can i get this document to an md file with styling intact?
Copilot said:
Copilot

Good question — and you’re right to care about styling staying intact. The answer depends on where you want the .md file to live, but the good news is: your document is already valid Markdown. You just need to get it out of the Page and into a file without the platform “helping” too much.

Below are three reliable ways, ranked from cleanest to most tool‑agnostic.

Option 1 (Cleanest): Copy → Save as .md (recommended)

This preserves all formatting exactly as written and gives you full control.

Steps

  1. Select all content in the Page (Cmd+A / Ctrl+A)
  2. Copy (Cmd+C / Ctrl+C)
  3. Open a plain‑text editor:
    • VS Code ✅ (best)
    • Obsidian ✅
    • Typora ✅
    • Notepad++ ✅
    • Even basic Notepad/TextEdit (plain text mode)
  4. Paste the content
  5. Save the file as: 
    edge-workspace-naming-and-color-system.md

Why this works

  • Your document already uses:
    • Markdown headings (#, ##)
    • Tables
    • Fenced code blocks
    • Inline emphasis
  • No conversion step = no syntax loss
  • Editors like VS Code or Obsidian will render it instantly

✅ This is the method most second‑brain users stick with long‑term

Option 2: Export if your platform supports “Markdown export”

Some Page/Canvas environments offer Export → Markdown or Download → .md.

If available:

  • Use Export / Download
  • Choose Markdown (.md)

Caveats

  • Some tools:
    • Rewrite tables
    • Change code‑block languages
    • Collapse spacing
  • If the export preview doesn’t look identical, abort and use Option 1

✅ Use only if you verify the output is byte‑for‑byte sane

Option 3: Add YAML frontmatter, then save (Obsidian / Logseq style)

If you want this to be first‑class in your second brain, add frontmatter before exporting.

Example (optional but powerful)

Add this at the very top:

YAML
---
type: convention
domain: browser
tool: microsoft-edge
status: stable
created: 2026-05-14
---

Then use Option 1 to save it as .md.

Why this helps

  • Enables:
    • Filtering
    • Backlinks
    • Status tracking
  • Zero impact on Markdown rendering
  • Safe in Obsidian, Logseq, Marked, VS Code, GitHub

What not to do

🚫 Don’t export as:

  • PDF (lossy, dead format)
  • Word (.docx)
  • HTML → Markdown (adds noise)
  • Rich Text / RTF

🚫 Don’t rely on “smart editors” that auto‑reformat tables or code blocks unless you trust them.

Quick validation checklist (30 seconds)

After saving:

  • ✅ Headings render properly
  • ✅ Tables align correctly
  • ✅ Code blocks stay fenced
  • ✅ Emojis/colors remain inline
  • ✅ No extra backticks or escaped chars

If all yes → you’re done.

If you want, next we can:

  • Normalize this for GitHub wiki style
  • Split it into:
    • conventions/edge-workspaces.md
    • examples/edge-workspaces.md
  • Align it with:
    • VS Code workspace naming
    • Repo naming
    • Folder structure
  • Create a “Quick Create” snippet you can paste when making a new workspace

Just tell me where this .md file is going (Obsidian vault, Git repo, Notion export, etc.) and I’ll tailor it exactly.
